draft.introduction: Hesychius of Sinai (eighth or ninth century) was a monk and priest at the Monastery of Saint Catherine on Mount Sinai. His treatise On Watchfulness and Holiness (Peri Nepseos kai Aretes) survives in two hundred brief chapters, collected in the Philokalia. It is one of the foundational texts of the Hesychast tradition—the practice of inner stillness (hesychia) through the continuous repetition of the Jesus Prayer. Hesychius teaches that the mind must be kept in a state of constant vigilance (nepsis), watching the thoughts as they arise and rejecting those that come from the demons. The primary weapon in this battle is the invocation of the name of Jesus: “Lord Jesus Christ, Son of God, have mercy on me.” Through unceasing repetition, this prayer descends from the mind into the heart, and the practitioner enters a state of inner stillness in which the divine light becomes perceptible. This is theosis as attention: the concentrated, unbroken focus of the mind on God, sustained by a single sacred formula, leading to the transformation of the entire person.

translation.text: Watchfulness is a spiritual art which, if diligently practised over a long period, completely frees us, with God’s help, from impassioned thoughts, impassioned words, and evil actions. It gives us a sure knowledge of the incomprehensible God, so far as this is possible, and a solution of divine and hidden mysteries.

Watchfulness is a continual fixing and halting of thought at the door of the heart. In this way predatory and murderous thoughts are marked down as they approach and what they say and do is noted; and we can see in what way the demonic phantoms try to deceive the intellect.
translation.original_text: Νῆψίς ἐστι νοὸς προσεδρεύων τῇ θύρᾳ τῆς καρδίας

translation.text: Let the name of Jesus cling to your breath, and then you will know the value of stillness (hesychia).
translation.original_text: Κολλάσθω τῇ πνοῇ σου τὸ ὄνομα τοῦ Ἰησοῦ

translation.text: The heart that has been completely emptied of mental images gives birth to divine and mysterious intellections that play within it like fish and dolphins in a calm sea. The sea is fanned by a soft wind, and the heart by the Holy Spirit.

Through the Jesus Prayer the intellect is purified of every image and illuminated by the divine light. The mind that has been united with the name of Jesus becomes radiant, and through this radiance the whole person is transformed into the likeness of God.
translation.original_text: Διὰ τῆς τοῦ Ἰησοῦ προσευχῆς ὁ νοῦς καθαίρεται

translation.text: The name of Jesus, when clung to by the intellect and planted in the heart, fills us with the grace of the Holy Spirit. The heart that has tasted the sweetness of this name can never again be separated from it. Through it, the passions are consumed, the demons are driven out, and the divine light shines forth within the soul.
translation.original_text: Τὸ ὄνομα τοῦ Ἰησοῦ κολλώμενον τῷ νῷ

translator_notes.note: The image of the intellect standing guard “at the door of the heart” comes from Proverbs 4:23 (“Guard your heart with all diligence”) and became the central metaphor of Hesychast practice. The “heart” in this tradition is not the physical organ or the seat of emotions but the spiritual centre of the person—the place where the intellect, the will, and divine grace converge.

The instruction to attach the name of Jesus to the breath is the earliest explicit connection between the Jesus Prayer and the breathing techniques that would later become central to Hesychast practice. By the fourteenth century, detailed instructions for coordinating the prayer with inhalation and exhalation, and with a particular bodily posture, would be formalized by Gregory of Sinai and defended by Gregory Palamas.

The image of divine intellections playing in the purified heart “like fish and dolphins in a calm sea” is one of the most beautiful in the entire Philokalia. It suggests that the goal of watchfulness is not emptiness but a new kind of fullness—a mind cleared of demonic phantoms and filled instead with the living movements of the Holy Spirit.
